rulings.Case Title: GARY DWAYNE FAW et al VS. UNITED PARCEL SERVICE OF AMERICA, INC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Responses By Plaintiffs Gary And Jenifer Faw To Supplemental Interrogatories And Supplemental Request For Production Of Documents, Set 1; Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,185
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday March 3, 2015, Line 1, DEFENDANT UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC'S Motion To Compel Responses By Plaintiffs Gary And Jenifer Faw To Supplemental Interrogatories And Supplemental Request For Production Of Documents, Set 1; Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,185. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, no opposition filed. Objection-free responses to discovery are due within 10 days of service of this order. Sanctions awarded against plaintiffs in the amount of $1,185, to be paid within 10 days of service of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to chuck@paoligeerhart.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DONNA RODRIGUEZ VS. DTZ, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alt Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 3, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANTS DTZ AMERICAS, INC. AND CHRISTOPHER JOHNKE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Motion for summary judgment granted. The Court finds good cause for hearing this motion within 30 days of trial. The Declaration of Robert M. Pattison establishes that the motion was scheduled on the first available hearing date. As to causes of action 1-6, Defendants met their initial burden of showing that Plaintiff cannot establish any nexus between her race and any perceived harassment, discrimination, or retaliation. (Defendant's Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF") 1-52.) Defendants have also met their initial burden by showing that there were legitimate, non-discriminatory reasons for any action taken against Plaintiff. (UMF 8-10, 12, 16-19, 35, 38.) Plaintiff has not met her burden of producing "substantial responsive evidence that the employer's showing was untrue or pretextual." (Hersant v. California Dept. of Social Services (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 997, 1004-05.) Plaintiff admits in her deposition that she did not overhear her coworkers make statements about her race. (UMF 48.) Additionally, the Declaration of Bridget Wayne is insufficient to raise an inference that there is a nexus between any alleged harassment, discrimination, or retaliation, and Plaintiff's race. Wayne's statement that she overhead Christopher Johnke state that Plaintiff is a Mexican is not substantial evidence of a nexus. (See Declaration of Bridget Wayne, par. 7.) No context for the statement is provided. Further, it is undisputed that Johnke did not play a role in Plaintiff's termination or placement on a performance improvement plan. (UMF 20-21.) As to the 8th cause of action for failure to pay wages, Defendants met their initial burden of showing that Plaintiff cannot establish that she worked overtime. (UMF 58.) Plaintiff does not dispute the fact that she worked "from around 8:30 or 9 in the morning until 5 or 6 at night" and has therefore not raised a triable issue of material fact as to whether Defendants failed to pay overtime. As to the 9th cause of action for failure to provide meal and rest breaks, Defendants met their initial burden establishing that the company provides meal and rest breaks and that Plaintiff was never instructed not to take a meal or rest break. (UMF 59.) Plaintiff does not raise a triable issue of material fact. Brinker Restaurant Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 1004 holds that the employer must provide the opportunity for a lunch and rest break, but that the employer does not have to ensure that the employee takes a break. As to the 7th and 10th causes of action, these causes of action are derivative of the other wage and hour causes of action, and therefore summary adjudication as to these causes of action is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prev ailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KENDRA L. TANACEA VS. RICHARD R. TAVERNETTI MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, Md, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two, And For Order Striking Objections; Request Questions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 3, 2015, Line 5, PLAINTIFF KENDRA TANACEA'S Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, MD, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two, And For Order Striking Objections; Request Questions. (PART 1 OF 2) Granted. Objection as to privacy is overruled. Personal financial information comes within the zone of privacy protected by Article I, § 1 of the California Constitution. Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court (1975) 15 Cal. 3d 652, 656 Tax records are also specifically protected by statute and case law. California Revenue and Tax Code §19542 et seq.; Webb v. Standard Oil Co. (1957) 49 Cal. 2d. 509 When an individual's right of privacy in his financial affairs conflicts with the public need for discovery in litigation, the competing interests must be carefully balanced. Moskowitz v. Superior Court 137 C.A. 3d 313,316 citing Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Court, supra. The more sensitive the information the greater the need for discovery must be shown. Hoffman Corp. v. Superior Court (1985) 172 C. A. 3d 357 Even where the balance weighs in favor of disclosure of private information, the scope of the disclosure will be narrowly circumscribed; such an invasion of the right of privacy "must be drawn with narrow specificity" and is permitted only to the extent necessary for a fair resolution of the lawsuit. Moskowitz v. Superior Court, supra, citing Britt v. Superior Court 20 Cal. 3d 844 The case law is clear--where a party is compelled to reveal financial information because the information is relevant to the subject matter of the litigation, the court should take "a closer look at the requests with an eye toward limiting discovery to those documents where are reasonably related to lead to admissible evidence of [plaintiffs'] substantive claims." Rawnsley v. Superior Court (1986) 183 Cal. App. 3d 86. The present lawsuit involves allegations that defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, M.D. recommended a hip replacement device, which he had a financial interest in. In an attempt to prove the allegations, plaintiff must be able to obtain documents showing his compensation from his consulting agreement with Exactech, Inc. The documents produced shall be subject to a protective order and shall be returned and/or destroyed within 30 days of the resolution of this case or when all appeals have been exhausted. All other objections are overruled on their merits. The court finds that the responses to the requests are not in compliance with C.C.P. § 2031.210 - 240. Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, M.D. to serve a verified response and produce documents to Requests No. 1-9 no later than March 17, 2015. Response to be in compliance with C.C.P. § 2031.210 et seq. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is granted. Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, M.D. to pay sanctions in the amount of $3600.00 no later than March 17, 2015. Check to be made payable to Kendra L. Tanacea and her attorneys of record Injijian Law Office, APC. (CONTINUED IN PART 2 OF 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KENDRA L. TANACEA VS. RICHARD R. TAVERNETTI MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, Md, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs Special Interrogatories, Set One, And For Order Striking Objections; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 3, 2015, Line 5, PLAINTIFF KENDRA TANACEA'S Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, MD, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs Special Interrogatories, Set One, And For Order Striking Objections; Request For Sanctions. (PART 1 OF 2) Granted. Privacy objection is overruled. The case law is clear--where a party is compelled to reveal financial information because the information is relevant to the subject matter of the litigation, the court should take "a closer look at the requests with an eye toward limiting discovery to those documents where are reasonably related to lead to admissible evidence of [plaintiffs'] substantive claims." Rawnsley v. Superior Court (1986) 183 Cal. App. 3d 86. The present lawsuit involves allegations that defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, Md. recommended a hip replacement device, which he had a financial interest in. In an attempt to prove the allegations, plaintiff must be able to discovery information showing defendant Tavernetti's compensation from his consulting agreement with Exactech, Inc. All other objections are overruled on their merits. The court further finds that defendant Tavernetti did not make the required good faith effort in responding to the interrogatories. A party must make a reasonable and good faith effort to obtain the information in answering interrogatories. Regency Health Services, Inc. v. Superior Court (1998) 64 CA4th1496 "A party cannot plead ignorance to information which can be obtained from sources under his control." Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 CA3d 771,782. This includes a party's lawyer Smith v. Sup. Ct (Alfred) (1961) 189 CA 2d 6, agents or employees Gordon v. Sup. Ct. (U.Z.MFG.Co) (1984) 161 CA 3d 15,167-168, family members Jones v. Superior Court (Benny) (1981) 119 CA 3d 534, 552 and experts who have been retained by a party and designated as a trial witness. Sigerseth v. Superior Court (1972) 23 CA 3d 427,433. See Weil and Brown, Cal Prac. Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2009) Par. 8:1051-1060. The statutes and the case law make it very clear that a party and the attorney must be proactive in obtaining the information to respond to the interrogatories. See Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 CA4th 390. Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, M.D. to serve verified responses to Specially Prepared Interrogatories No. 1-20 no later than March 17, 2015. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is granted. Defendant Richard R. Tavernetti, M.D. to pay sanctions in the amount of $4800.00 no later than March 17, 2015. Check to be made payable to Kendra L. Tanacea and her attorneys of record Injijian Law Office, APC. (CONTINUED IN PART 2 OF 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID GOLDFARB et al VS. TRINITY PROPERTIES et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs David Goldfarb And Giovanna Desimone-Goldfarb'S Motion To Quash And Or Modify Nine Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday March 3, 2015, Line 3, Plaintiffs David Goldfarb And Giovanna Desimone-Goldfarb's Motion To Quash And Or Modify Nine Subpoenas. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties are to meet and confer before the hearing to discuss: 1) what physical injuries are claimed and to what parts of the body, so that appropriately limited subpoenas can be issued pursuant to Britt; 2) are plaintiffs willing to stipulate that any emotional distress damages will only be that degree of emotional distress that would ordinarily be associated with the physical injuries suffered (i.e., will plaintiffs be claiming psychiatric injury and treatment, or not?); 3) if plaintiffs are claiming psychiatric injury, what is the specific type of injury claimed (e.g., PTSD) and 4) what categories of records in the possession of the CPA would be directly relevant to the claim or defense of the financial loss claim? =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ARKADY BERGER VS. GARY VARUM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify The Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records Directed To Pat Mclaughlin
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday March 3, 2015, Line 5, DEFENDANT GARY VARUM, ALEX VARUM, CHARLES THIEL, NATALIE KREIGEL, 1122 UNIVERSITY AVENUE, L.L.C, CALIFORNIA FAMILY COMPANY, LLC, CWR HOLDINGS, LLC, IRINA VARUM'S Motion To Quash Or Modify The Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records Directed To Pat Mclaughlin. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion denied. The Court finds that there is good cause for the discovery sought by the subpoenas. Sanctions denied as the request does not clearly identify against whom sanctions are sought. See CCP sec. § 2023.040, addressing notice requirements for sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to chuck@paoligeerhart.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD LEICHUS VS. CHRISTOPHER LEICHUS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 3, 2015, Line 11, DEFENDANTS CHRISTOPHER LEICHUS AND MARLENE LEICHUS' MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE. Continued to April 3, 2015 to give all parties the opportunity to comply with Local Rule 2.6B. All parties must deliver courtesy copies of the papers they filed to Department 302, with a cover letter stating the new hearing date, no later than March 3, 2015 at 4:00 pm. Any exhibit must be separated by an exhibit tab per CRC 3.1110(f). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EHG)
